text: The patient is positioned in lithotomy for a laparoscopic duodenal switch. The surgeon performs a sleeve gastrectomy, mobilizing the greater curvature approximately six centimeters from the pylorus. A loose sleeve is created over a 40 French bougie, with green load staple line reinforcement, ensuring the incisura angularis is not impinged. The fundus is completely removed.

The surgeon stands between the patient's legs and mobilizes the first and second portions of the duodenum, identifying the pylorus and carefully dissecting around the duodenum without causing injury. Approximately four to five centimeters of the postpyloric duodenum are mobilized with antral traction, creating a plane lateral to the common bile duct. An articulating sector is used to create a retro duodenal tunnel, and a pendular strain facilitates further mobilization. The duodenum is elevated and transected using a blue load stapler with reinforcement. The surgeon then moves to the left of the patient, identifies the ileocecal junction, and rotates the bowel in an anti-clockwise fashion, marking it at 125 centimeters to signify the common channel length, and counts another 125 centimeters while maintaining the rotation.

The surgeon divides the bowel at 250 centimeters using a white load stapler, followed by division of the mesentery. The biliary limb is marked with a clip, and the Roux limb is rotated to align with the common channel. An H-shaped anastomosis is created using bidirectional firing with white load staplers to prevent narrowing. The common enterotomy is closed transversely, and the mesenteric defect is exposed and closed with a running 2-0 silk stitch. The surgeon then positions between the patient's legs to take the Roux limb in an anti-colic fashion.

The surgeon creates a retrocolic tunnel and places a stay suture to align the small bowel with the post-pyloric duodenum. A posterior running suture is performed using a two-oh vicryl stitch to complete the posterior row of the anastomosis. The duodenum and ileum are opened using a harmonic scalpel, which seals the tissue to prevent mucosal eversion. A second layer of sutures is placed to ensure a wide anastomosis.

The patient is positioned in lithotomy with diamond configuration port placement for the laparoscopic procedure. Six trocars are used during the surgery. The surgeon runs sutures from superior to inferior to tie the anastomosis, followed by a second anterior layer to approximate the serosa. The anastomosis is examined using an endoscope or methylene blue test, and a drain is placed around the anastomosis, which is removed on post-op day three.
